TRANSCRIPT
PROMOTING OTHER PEOPLE’S LAUNCHES
PART 2
TRANSCRIPT
This is the second video on how to promote other people's launches. This one's, we're
going to go strategy here. I always love to talk strategy. To start with the big picture first, we'll fill
in the details later. Big picture strategy. The number one question is: What to promote?
What do you want to promote? What launches do you want to promote? I'll tell you this, as you
start to promote stuff, and the word starts to spread that you have the capability to promote
stuff and make sales, then you'll have more and more people coming to you asking you to
promote. Maybe when you're starting out, it's not like the world's beating a path to your door
but they will be very soon. I just want to talk about some of my considerations when I look to
promote. I think they'd be great considerations for you.
First of all, does it fit for your list? For instance, my list is a bunch of
entrepreneurs and people that want to be entrepreneurs. I had this guy who just kept on
pounding me and pounding me. He wanted to create a product about learning to play the guitar.
I like playing the guitar. I love learning about playing the guitar. Just because I do doesn't mean
it's a good fit for my list. I'm like, "No. This does not work." He's like, "Well, you've got this huge
list and surely at least a few of them are interested in playing the guitar." Yes, that's an accurate
statement, but only a tiny bit. I'm going to be mailing to my entire list, and they're going to be
like, "Why is Jeff sending me stuff about learning to play guitar? It totally doesn't fit with why I
joined this list, why I signed up with him, why I'm following him." You have to figure out does it
fit your list.
For me, stuff about building your business fits my list. Stuff about being productive,
about some certain amount of personal development, does fit my list. Specifically stuff about
becoming an expert, selling information, about eCommerce … well, those are entrepreneurs,
right? Yeah, but it doesn't fit my list so well. You have to really understand who is on
your list, why they're on your list, and then look at the products to see if they fit
your list. Like I said, sometimes you can be tangential. I just very successfully promoted a
product about productivity, about personal productivity. It's not exactly what people signed up
for, but it certainly fits with an entrepreneur. They all want to be more productive.
Next is, is it a strategic opportunity for you or is it just a money maker? It's
fine to sometimes just do pure money makers, but is it strategic? Does it have bigger payouts?
Will it build relationships for you within your industry with the person you're promoting for?
Those are a couple key things that are possible to create strategic reasons to promote. Now

PROMOTING OTHER PEOPLE’S LAUNCHES
PART 2
another one, and this might sound funny, but the question is, will they actually
pay you? If you promote for them and you actually make sales, are they actually going to pay
you? It might sound weird, but there have been plenty of situations that I know of, in fact there's
been some where I've been involved where you promote for someone, you make sales, and then
they go out of business or they just forget to pay you or they don't track for you or just all kinds
of weirdness can happen.
This industry can sort of be the wild west. In fact, if you think back to that last video
where I invited you to be an affiliate for Product Launch Formula, I even said, "We pay on time
every month, and we've done so for 10 years." The reason I said that is there are a lot of
people that don't pay you on time or just don't pay you. That's one of the
considerations you need is when you're looking at someone, it's like, "How much
do I trust this person?" I don't want to scare you off, but it's something to think about.
Another one is will the offer convert? You're here to promote for this launch. Is it
going to be ... have they done an internal launch? Have they launched the product before? Have
they tested the offer? Do they have the capability, the marketing chops to put
together a good launch? Is it their first rodeo? If it's the first time they've ever done a launch
or if it's the first time they've ever put out this offer … if they've never done an internal launch,
it's something to think about. Again, that's why in that last video I said, "Product Launch
Formula, it's proven. It's been proven for 10 years." You want to look for the same thing. You
want to go with someone where you know they're going to put together a process. If you send
them traffic, it's going to convert.
Another huge one is will they take care of your people? You're going to send
your list here and the people on your list are going to go judge you based on where you send
them. If you send them into some series of pre-launch videos but there's no value in those
videos, they will judge you negatively. If they buy the product and the product doesn't
deliver on the promise, they will judge you negatively. You're going to be judged.
It's like your mom told you when you were growing up, "You're going to be judged by the people
you hang out with." It is a reality. You will be judged by who you hang out with. That goes for if
you're mailing into a promotion, you're going to be judge by those people and how they treat
your list and your prospects and your clients will reflect on you.
If you send them to someone who gives great value and gives them a great product, then
they will love you even more for that. They will appreciate that referral. I think about it like,
"Will they take care of my people if they end up buying the product, but also will

PROMOTING OTHER PEOPLE’S LAUNCHES
PART 2
they take care of my people if they don't buy the product." Just through the pre-
launch process, are they going to build value? Are they going to treat them respectfully? Are they
going to beat them over the head and is it going to end up feeling like they were at some bad
time share pitch? You want them to respect your people. Definitely something to think about.
Another one is do you love the product? I have lots of promotions that I just crushed it in
because I loved the product. For instance, Eben Pagen just rolled out a product called "Wake Up
Productive." I love the product. I went through it. When he first released it years ago, it made a
profound difference in my life, profound. I still to this day use what I learned in that product
nearly every day or the year. I'd say 80% of the days during the year I use what I learned in his
product. I went thought the product probably four or five years ago. It still makes an impact. I
love the product. I love promoting it. That's a big one. Do you really love the product? Do
you love the person, the company that's putting the product out?
Then there's this whole idea that someone you really want to get their attention. I'm
talking about the product producer, that company, whoever that person is. Do you want to
get on their radar? Does it fit with your list? Can you promote that thing? If you
sell a bunch of it, you will get on their radar. Is it someone you want to get their
attention? You want to build a warmer relationship, a closer relationship.
Now another thing I want to talk about is let's call it big picture, let's call it leaderboard
games. As I mentioned in that last video, in many Joint Venture launches there are these
leaderboards. The leaderboard is, I'll put out a leaderboard every single day during my launch
and it'll be, I'll just have a list of all the way through number usually 20, sometimes 25, and it
might be Joe, Frank, Bob ... I'll list in order where everyone is in terms of how many sales
they've made.
It ends up being a real competition kind of thing. Let's not be sexist here, let's put in
Sally there as well. That's awesome because I've got a lot of women. Back in 2005, 2006 it was
all men, 100% men. Then in 2008 we started to see the women starting to creep in at the bottom
of the leaderboard. Now we've got the women breaking up into the top of the leaderboard and
that's awesome. I love it. That's one great change that has come about in this industry is not too
long ago there were not very many female leaders publishing in this industry, but now we've got
a lot of them and they are on the upswing and they're doing big huge things. In fact, to my
knowledge I can think of a couple women that have done better in JV launches than literally
anyone in the history of the industry. That's awesome.

PROMOTING OTHER PEOPLE’S LAUNCHES
PART 2
Anyway, I put out this leaderboard. I put one out every day during my launch. Most
times in a big JV launches, the person doing the launch will put out these
leaderboards. This can be a very significant thing for you once you start
promoting for launches. One distinction, there's often a leaderboard in the prelaunch before
the open cart. There's often a leaderboard just for leads. Who's sent the most leads in the
prelaunch? Sometimes there's prizes for the leads. Then there's a leaderboard for the sales.
Personally for me, when I'm promoting for someone, I don't really care about how I do in that
lead’s leaderboard because it's all about, really it's about the sales. Just throw that in there.
There is a lead’s leaderboard for whose sent the most opt-ins and there's a sales leaderboard for
whose sent the most sales.
Now the thing is, if you're just starting out in the industry and all of a sudden you start
showing up, you're down here at number 16 or something and here's you, all the sudden people
are like, "Wow I've never heard of this person. Who is this person?" Then this is a way to start to
get attention within your industry, build positioning. As you move up the leaderboard,
you get more attention. I guarantee, you start hitting the top 10, then you really
start to get attention. My leaderboard tends to be, because I get the most support of pretty
much anyone in the industry just because I've helped out a lot of people and PLF is awesome,
I've got a really strong leaderboard. It tends to ... getting in the top 10 of my leaderboard isn't
easy. If you get in the top 10 of my leaderboard or some of the major launches, then you will
start to get some real attention.
You get close to the top five and people are going to start calling you up.
They're going to want to ... you'll get into the deal flow. This is how you can get
positioning no matter what industry you're in. If there are these big JV launches and
they're putting out a leaderboard, this is the way you start to get noticed. That's sort of just
general, industry-wide attention, but whoever the person is doing this launch, whoever the
entrepreneur is, whoever's doing it ... I'll tell you what. As you move up their leaderboard, you
will start to get their attention. Like I said, get in my top 10 and I know who you are. You get in
my top five, and it's like, "Holy cow. This is a potential long-term business relationship that I
want to foster."
Now one of the things you want to do is if you want to play the leaderboard game and try
to game your way into doing well on the leaderboard, that's a great thing to do. It's a great way
to get attention with your industry. You want to pick your battles. Like I mentioned, often
during the prelaunch there'll be a leaderboard for the number of opt-ins, for the number of
leads. Sometimes like in our launches, I'll do a special leaderboard just for over a two day period

PROMOTING OTHER PEOPLE’S LAUNCHES
PART 2
or maybe for video two, whoever sends the most leads for video two. We'll do a little mini-
leaderboard just for that. Maybe I'll do a mini-leaderboard for the last day of the launch or
something like that. This is something I invented. I call them their sprint bonuses. Frankly it
was named after bicycle racing. I got the idea from the Tour de France. They have sprint
bonuses. It's like a race within the race.
Often on the leaderboards, there'll be a leaderboard within the leaderboard, or race
within the race. Those can be easier to win. If I do a leaderboard just for video two, number of
opt-ins just for video two, that could be easier for you to get on than if it was for the overall
leaderboard for sales or the overall leaderboard for leads. There could be some launches that
just aren't getting as much support. You could look in and say, "Wow. They're halfway through
this launch and they don't have that many big names up there ahead of me. Maybe this is where
I'm going to go and just go all out and try to do really well." We'll talk about this later when we
get to tactics. There's ways you can really boost your position on these
leaderboards by maybe putting together some amazing bonus package.
You can say, pick out one launch and say, "This is the one launch where I want to do
good and I want to hit top 10 on this leaderboard, so I'm going to go all out. I'm going to put
together a special promotion," like we'll talk about in the next video. "I'm going to put together a
special bonus," like we'll talk about in the next two videos, "and just go all out to try to get top
10." I'll often do this. I'll go in and I'll be, "I want to be top five or I want to be number one or I
want to be top three." You'll pick your battles and you'll look for where you really want to
perform to get people's attention, whether it's the person doing the launch or just attention
within the industry.
